claim: Provider configuration in demand evidence dispatch uses provider-specific CLI argument patterns and does not pass tool restriction flags to any provider, relying on boundary enforcement instead. This claim is CONFIRMED by independent verification.

evidence.summary: Codex provider invocation uses --cd, --sandbox, -c for approval_policy, --output-last-message; NO --tools, --allowedTools, or --disallowedTools parameters exist in Codex dispatch contract.
evidence.why: Directly contradicts any assumption that Codex has tool restriction APIs; confirms provider-specific CLI structure.

evidence.summary: Custom provider invocation uses shell template with ${model} substitution; NO tool restriction parameters in custom dispatch contract.
evidence.why: Confirms custom provider has no tool configuration capabilities, only command templating.

evidence.summary: Claude provider invocation accepts --tools, --allowedTools, --disallowedTools, --permission-mode flags, unlike Codex and custom.
evidence.why: Shows Claude uniquely has tool restriction parameters in its dispatch contract.

evidence.summary: executionConfig clears ALL Claude tool restriction flags: settings='', claude_tools='', claude_allowed_tools='', claude_disallowed_tools='', claude_no_session_persistence=true.
evidence.why: Evidence mode explicitly strips Claude tool flags regardless of config.ai input, preventing cross-provider flag leakage.

evidence.summary: Codex provider special case: if provider is codex and config.ai.model contains 'claude', model field is cleared to avoid cross-provider model pollution.
evidence.why: Explicit handling prevents Claude default model assumptions from leaking into Codex dispatch.

evidence.summary: Tool profile normalization: research/fetch/web/web-research normalize to 'research'; full/all/write/execution normalize to 'full' only with allowFullAgentTools=true; default is 'boundary'.
evidence.why: Three distinct tool profiles exist with explicit authorization gates for full mode.

evidence.summary: Boundary violations after agent execution result in BOUNDARY_PROJECT_MUTATION blockers and force prd_ready=false.
evidence.why: Boundary enforcement is provider-agnostic and hard blocks violations regardless of which provider caused mutations.

evidence.summary: Test verifies Codex dispatch does not inherit Claude default model and does not force read-only sandbox assumptions.
evidence.why: Test evidence confirms provider isolation and model clearing logic works as claimed.

evidence.summary: Test verifies Claude dispatch clears tool restriction flags and relies on boundary enforcement instead.
evidence.why: Test evidence confirms Claude tool flags are empty in evidence mode, matching the clearing logic at lines 456-462.

evidence.summary: buildBoundarySnapshot and diffBoundarySnapshots implement provider-agnostic git-backed file mutation detection with allowed roots exclusion.
evidence.why: Boundary enforcement works by comparing before/after file digests regardless of which provider was used.

assumptions: Codex and custom providers do not have tool restriction APIs equivalent to Claude's --tools/allowedTools/disallowedTools flags.
Boundary enforcement assumes git is available and working for git-backed snapshot; falls back to filesystem walk otherwise.
Provider command availability (codex, claude CLI on PATH) is assumed but verified by agent-contract inspector before execution.
risks: If a provider other than Claude gains tool restriction flags, the current clearing logic (lines 456-462) would not apply to them.
If git is not available or repository state is dirty, boundary detection may miss changes or have false positives.
Model value clearing for Codex (line 448-449) uses string matching 'claude' which could be overly aggressive or miss other model patterns.

result.notes: Explorer claim is CORRECT. Independent verification confirms provider-specific CLI patterns, Claude tool flag clearing, and provider-agnostic boundary enforcement. No conflicts found between evidence and implementation.
